required.title: [Read/Listen] Authentic target language texts with audio
[Reference] IPA chart and target language phonological description

supplementary.title: [Listen] Regional dialect recordings in target language

competence_markers.description: The student can identify and explain connected speech phenomena (assimilation, elision, reduction, linking) in their target language with specific examples
The student can sustain comprehension of natural-speed target language speech for at least 10 consecutive minutes and narrate the content
The student can describe at least two regional varieties of their target language and identify the systematic phonological differences between them
The student understands and can explain how pronunciation functions as a social signal (formality, identity, class, region) in the target language
The student can read an authentic target language text aloud with pronunciation that captures connected speech patterns, not just citation forms
The student demonstrates self-monitoring capability -- can identify errors in their own recorded speech and describe strategies for correction
The student maintains an extensive listening practice of at least 3 hours per week and can document improvement in comprehension over time
For tonal languages: the student produces correct tone sandhi patterns in connected speech, not just isolated words

narration_prompts.prompt: What is connected speech, and why does it make natural-speed listening so much harder than textbook listening?
narration_prompts.detail: Give at least three specific examples of connected speech phenomena from your target language.

narration_prompts.prompt: How has your listening comprehension changed over the course of this packet?
narration_prompts.detail: What could you not understand at the beginning that you can understand now? What strategies helped most?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe the regional variation in your target language.
narration_prompts.detail: What are the major dialectal differences? Which variety are you learning and why? How do you respond when you encounter a different variety?

narration_prompts.prompt: How does pronunciation function as a social signal in your target language?
narration_prompts.detail: Give specific examples of how phonological choices communicate formality, identity, or social position.

narration_prompts.prompt: Play me a recording of yourself from early in this packet and one from now.
narration_prompts.detail: What differences do you hear? What has improved? What still needs work?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest phonological feature of your target language for you right now?
narration_prompts.detail: Why is it hard? What is your strategy for mastering it? Where are you on the self-monitoring progression for this feature?

cross_connections.description: The cultural pragmatics of pronunciation studied here connect directly to the broader communication norms framework in 3.1.5. Pronunciation is one channel through which cultural communication norms are enacted. The student who understands both phonological and cultural pragmatics communicates with genuine competence, not just technical accuracy.

The connected speech comprehension developed here makes the survival vocabulary from 3.1.3 actually usable -- the student who can understand natural-speed responses to their carefully produced phrases has crossed from rehearsed phrases to real communication.

Structural analysis and phonological analysis converge in this packet. The student who understands both the grammatical structure and the sound system of their target language has a comprehensive map of the language's architecture.

The sociolinguistic awareness developed here -- that pronunciation carries social meaning, that register shifts are phonological as well as lexical -- connects directly to the rhetorical competence developed in 3.3. The persuasive speaker commands phonological register as well as verbal content.

Dialect variation and language attitudes are products of social history. The student who understands why certain pronunciations are stigmatized and others are prestigious is engaging with the same power structures studied in 1.1.3.

Extended listening at the edge of comprehension is sustained productive frustration. The student who persists through the phase where natural-speed speech is overwhelming and emerges with comprehension has built resilience that transfers across every challenging endeavor.

content.why_this_matters:
You can now read the IPA. You can describe any consonant by its place, manner, and voicing. You understand why your ears fail to hear certain contrasts and what to do about it. You have been shadowing native speech daily and your pronunciation is measurably better than when you started B1.

Now comes the harder work: taking those analytical tools into sustained, authentic communication. The difference between B1 and B2 is the difference between understanding the mechanics of swimming and actually swimming in open water. You have the mechanics. Now you swim.

In this packet, you will engage with extended authentic speech -- not graded textbook recordings but real conversations, news broadcasts, stories, and arguments produced by native speakers for native speakers. You will encounter connected speech phenomena -- the way sounds change, blend, disappear, and emerge when people talk at natural speed. You will develop the ability to comprehend natural-speed speech in your target language, not just slow, carefully enunciated textbook audio. And you will begin to understand something that no IPA chart can teach you: that pronunciation is not just about producing the right sounds -- it is about producing them in the right way for the right social context. The phonological choices you make carry cultural meaning.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Connected speech comprehension** -- understanding natural-speed speech with its assimilations, elisions, reductions, and linking
2. **Extended listening endurance** -- the ability to sustain comprehension over minutes and hours, not just seconds
3. **Authentic text engagement** -- reading and listening to material produced for native speakers, not language learners
4. **Sociolinguistic awareness** -- understanding that pronunciation varies by region, class, gender, age, and social context, and that these variations carry social meaning
5. **Cultural pragmatics of sound** -- how phonological choices signal identity, formality, solidarity, and intent
6. **Tone system depth** -- for students working with tonal languages, mastery of tone in connected speech, including tone sandhi
7. **Self-monitoring and self-correction** -- the ability to hear your own errors in real time and correct them
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Connected Speech -- Why Real Speech Sounds Nothing Like the Textbook

When you learned IPA transcriptions in B1, you transcribed words in isolation -- carefully, distinctly, one at a time. Native speakers do not talk like that. In real speech, words flow into each other, sounds change because of what is next to them, entire syllables disappear, and new sounds emerge at word boundaries that are not in any dictionary.

This is connected speech, and it is why students who understand every word in a textbook recording are lost when they hear the same language spoken naturally.

**Assimilation:** Sounds become more like their neighbors. In English, "ten bags" is typically pronounced "tem bags" -- the [n] becomes [m] because the following [b] is bilabial, and the [n] assimilates to match it. Your target language has its own assimilation patterns. Learning them is essential for comprehension.

**Elision:** Sounds disappear entirely. The "t" in English "castle" is silent for most speakers. The first "d" in "Wednesday" vanishes. Every language has elision patterns, and they are often the reason you cannot parse natural-speed speech -- sounds you expect to hear are simply not there.

**Reduction:** Unstressed syllables become shorter, quieter, and less distinct. In English, the word "to" in "I want to go" is typically pronounced [tə] or even [t] -- the vowel reduces to almost nothing. Many languages reduce unstressed syllables, and the specific reduction patterns are a major source of comprehension difficulty.

**Linking:** New sounds appear at word boundaries. In many English dialects, "law and order" is pronounced with an [r] between "law" and "and" -- the so-called intrusive [r]. French has extensive liaison rules where final consonants that are silent in isolation are pronounced when the next word begins with a vowel. Your target language's linking rules are rules you must learn to hear, or you will hear word boundaries in the wrong places.

**Tone sandhi (tonal languages):** In connected speech, tones change based on what tone comes next. Mandarin's third tone before another third tone becomes a second tone: "ni hao" (hello) is technically two third tones but is pronounced with a second tone followed by a third. These sandhi rules are systematic, but they mean that the tones you learned in isolation are not always the tones you hear in speech.

### Part 2: Extended Listening -- Building Endurance

Listening comprehension at natural speed is a skill that requires stamina. In B1, you worked with short clips and isolated examples. Now you need to build the ability to sustain comprehension over extended periods -- ten minutes, thirty minutes, an hour.

This is like physical endurance training. You build it gradually:

**Phase 1: Intensive listening with support.** Listen to a short passage (2-3 minutes) of natural-speed speech. Read the transcript while listening. Listen again without the transcript. Note where you lose comprehension. Identify the connected speech phenomena (assimilations, elisions, reductions) that caused the difficulty. Listen a third time and see if you can now hear what you missed. This is slow, careful work. You may spend 30 minutes on a 3-minute clip. That is correct.

**Phase 2: Extensive listening for gist.** Listen to longer passages (10-15 minutes) without a transcript. Do not try to understand every word. Listen for the main ideas, the topic shifts, the emotional tone. After listening, narrate what you understood. Then check against the transcript. The goal is to develop the ability to keep processing even when you miss parts -- a critical real-world skill.

**Phase 3: Sustained natural listening.** Listen to podcasts, radio, or audiobooks in the target language for 30-60 minutes. Comprehension will be partial, and that is expected. The goal is sustained engagement with natural-speed input. Your brain is processing patterns even when your conscious mind is not catching every word. This is how immersion works.

### Part 3: Sociolinguistic Variation -- There Is No Single "Correct" Pronunciation

Every language has dialects, accents, and pronunciation variations that are socially meaningful. The English you speak is not "English" -- it is a specific variety of English that tells listeners where you are from, what social class you likely belong to, how much education you have had, and what group you identify with.

Your target language is the same. There is no single "correct" pronunciation of Spanish -- there is Castilian Spanish, Mexican Spanish, Argentine Spanish, Caribbean Spanish, Andean Spanish, and many more, each with systematic phonological differences. The choice of which variety to learn is not just a practical decision -- it is a social one.

**Regional variation:** Dialects differ in predictable ways. The Spanish of Spain distinguishes [s] and [θ] (the "c" before "e" and "i" is pronounced differently from "s"). Most Latin American Spanish does not make this distinction -- both sounds are [s]. Neither is "more correct." They are different regional standards with equal legitimacy.

**Social variation:** Within any region, pronunciation varies by social class, education level, and social group. In many languages, certain pronunciations are associated with prestige (the "standard" or "educated" variety) while others are associated with solidarity and local identity. The student who learns only the prestige variety may sound formal or distant in casual settings. The student who learns only casual pronunciation may sound uneducated in formal settings.

**Age and gender variation:** Young speakers often pronounce things differently from older speakers -- not because they are sloppy but because language is always changing and younger generations lead the change. Gender-linked pronunciation differences exist in many languages (Japanese has distinct male and female speech patterns; Arabic has systematic gender-linked variation).

**Code-switching:** Many speakers switch between pronunciation varieties depending on context -- more standard at work, more local at home, more formal with strangers, more casual with friends. This is not inconsistency. It is sociolinguistic competence -- the ability to use pronunciation as a social tool.

### Part 4: Cultural Pragmatics of Sound

Pronunciation carries meaning beyond the dictionary definition of words. How you say something signals who you are, who you think you are talking to, and what kind of interaction you intend.

**Formality through phonology:** In many languages, careful, full pronunciation signals formality, while reduced, casual pronunciation signals intimacy. The Japanese speaker who pronounces every mora carefully is being formal. The one who blurs and reduces is being casual. The English speaker who says "going to" is being more formal than the one who says "gonna." These are phonological choices with social consequences.

**Identity through accent:** The accent you adopt in a second language signals something about your relationship to that language's culture. The American who speaks French with a flawless Parisian accent may be admired or may be seen as pretentious, depending on the context. The foreigner who retains a noticeable accent but speaks with correct grammar and appropriate pragmatics may be perceived as more authentic than one who performs native pronunciation. There is no single right answer here -- but there is a choice, and it should be a conscious one.

**Politeness through prosody:** Intonation patterns carry politeness information. A request spoken with rising intonation sounds more tentative and polite in many languages. A statement spoken with flat intonation may sound blunt or rude. The prosodic patterns of politeness are culturally specific and must be learned alongside the words themselves.

### Part 5: Authentic Text Engagement

An authentic text is anything produced by and for native speakers -- not simplified, not graded, not modified for learners. Engaging with authentic texts is where your phonological skills meet real language in all its complexity.

**Reading aloud from authentic texts:** Choose a passage from a target language newspaper, novel, or essay. Read it aloud. Record yourself. Compare to a native speaker reading the same text (or a similar one). Note where your pronunciation diverges. Are the differences in individual sounds, in rhythm, in intonation, or in connected speech phenomena? Each type of difference requires a different kind of practice.

**Transcribing natural speech:** Listen to a 30-second clip of natural-speed target language speech. Transcribe it in IPA (as best you can). Then check against the standard dictionary pronunciation of each word. Note every place where the natural speech differs from the dictionary form. These differences are the connected speech phenomena from Part 1 -- and transcribing them forces you to hear them.

**Engaging with multiple registers:** Listen to the same speaker in different contexts -- a news anchor reading the news versus being interviewed casually; a teacher in the classroom versus in the break room; a politician giving a speech versus speaking to their family. Note the phonological differences. The student who can hear register shifts in pronunciation is developing sociolinguistic competence.

### Part 6: Self-Monitoring and Self-Correction

The ultimate goal of phonological training is to internalize it -- to reach the point where you can hear your own errors in real time and correct them without conscious effort.

This develops in stages:
1. **Cannot hear the error at all** -- you produce an incorrect sound and do not notice.
2. **Can hear the error in recordings** -- when you listen to yourself played back, you catch the mistake.
3. **Can hear the error shortly after producing it** -- you say something wrong and realize it a moment later.
4. **Can hear the error in real time and self-correct** -- you catch the error as it happens and correct mid-sentence.
5. **Produce the correct form automatically** -- the error no longer occurs.

You will be at different stages for different sounds. Some target language sounds will already be at stage 5. Others may still be at stage 1. The work of this packet is to move every target sound as far along this progression as possible.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Connected Speech Analysis [First Two Weeks]
Select a 1-minute clip of natural-speed target language speech. Transcribe it in IPA. Identify every instance of assimilation, elision, reduction, and linking. Compare the connected speech forms to citation (dictionary) forms. Write up your analysis: what patterns do you see? Which connected speech phenomena are most frequent? Which cause you the most comprehension difficulty?

### Study 2: Extensive Listening Log [Ongoing]
Maintain a log of extensive listening practice: source, duration, estimated comprehension percentage, and notes on what you understood and what you missed. Aim for at least 3 hours per week of target language listening. Track comprehension percentage over time -- it should trend upward.

### Study 3: Dialect Comparison [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Select two regional varieties of your target language. Find audio samples of each. Produce a phonological comparison: what are the systematic differences in consonants, vowels, and prosody? Are there social or geographic factors that explain the differences? Which variety are you learning, and why? Written analysis of at least 500 words.

### Study 4: Register Shift Observation [Mid-Packet]
Find recordings of the same native speaker in at least two different social contexts (formal and casual). Document the phonological differences between registers: pronunciation changes, speed changes, reduction patterns, intonation shifts. What does this tell you about how pronunciation functions as a social signal?

### Study 5: Authentic Reading Practice [Ongoing from Mid-Packet]
Select a chapter or article in the target language each week. Read it aloud, record yourself, and listen back. Identify three specific pronunciation issues to work on. Practice those issues. Re-record at the end of the week. Compare recordings.

### Study 6: Tone Sandhi Mastery (Tonal Languages) [Mid-to-Late Packet]
If working with a tonal language, produce a comprehensive analysis of tone sandhi rules: what tones change in what environments? Practice sentences that trigger sandhi changes. Record yourself and verify that the sandhi patterns are correct. For non-tonal languages, substitute an analysis of intonation patterns in different sentence types (declarative, interrogative, imperative, exclamatory).

### Study 7: Self-Monitoring Assessment [Late Packet]
Record a 5-minute free conversation in the target language (with a native speaker or language partner). Listen to the recording and identify every pronunciation error you can detect. Classify each error by type (wrong phoneme, wrong allophone, wrong tone, wrong prosody, connected speech error). For each error, identify what stage of self-monitoring you are at (see Part 6). This is your phonological progress map.

### Study 8: Cultural Pragmatics Observation [Late Packet]
Observe (in person, on video, or in audio) a native speaker interaction in your target language. Document the phonological features that carry social or pragmatic meaning: formality signals, politeness intonation, identity markers, code-switching moments. Write an analysis of at least 400 words connecting phonological choices to social meaning.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Connected Speech Comprehension
**Developing well:** The student can follow natural-speed speech and identify when connected speech phenomena (assimilation, elision, reduction) cause comprehension difficulty. They can slow down a problematic passage, analyze what is happening phonologically, and then re-listen with improved comprehension.

**Developing slowly:** The student is overwhelmed by natural-speed speech and cannot identify why. They describe the problem as "they talk too fast" without analyzing the specific phonological causes.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough practice with natural-speed input? Are they doing the intensive listening exercises (transcribe, analyze, re-listen) or only extensive listening? The intensive work builds the analytical skills that make extensive listening productive.

### 2. Listening Endurance
**Developing well:** The student can sustain focused listening for 15+ minutes and narrate the main content afterward. Their weekly listening log shows increasing hours and increasing comprehension.

**Developing slowly:** The student loses focus after a few minutes of natural-speed speech. Listening log shows sporadic practice or consistently low comprehension.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the content interesting to the student? Boredom kills listening endurance faster than difficulty. Is the student trying material that is far too difficult? A comprehension rate below 50% for extended periods is demoralizing rather than productive. Step back to slightly easier content.

### 3. Sociolinguistic Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can identify different varieties of the target language by ear, describe the phonological differences, and explain the social factors that produce variation. They have a conscious relationship with the variety they are learning.

**Developing slowly:** The student does not notice variation or dismisses non-standard varieties as "wrong." They have not considered why they are learning the variety they are learning.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you exposed the student to multiple varieties? If they have only ever heard one dialect or register, they cannot develop awareness of variation.

### 4. Self-Monitoring
**Developing well:** When reviewing their own recordings, the student can identify specific errors and categorize them. In real-time conversation, they occasionally self-correct -- catching an error as they make it and repairing it. They can describe their current weaknesses precisely.

**Developing slowly:** The student cannot identify errors in their own recordings, or identifies everything as wrong without being able to specify what the issue is. No self-correction in conversation.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student doing the recording and comparison exercises? Self-monitoring develops through the practice of listening to yourself critically, not through conversation alone.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### All Extensive, No Intensive
Extensive listening (long periods of unanalyzed input) is necessary but not sufficient. Without intensive listening exercises (short passages analyzed in detail), the student absorbs input without understanding the phonological patterns that cause comprehension failure. Both are needed. A good balance is 70% extensive, 30% intensive.

### Perfectionism About Pronunciation
Some students become so focused on producing perfect sounds that they stop communicating. Pronunciation accuracy is important, but it serves communication, not the other way around. A student who speaks with minor pronunciation errors but communicates fluently and effectively is more advanced than a student who produces each sound perfectly but speaks in hesitant, disconnected phrases. Fluency and accuracy develop in parallel, not in sequence.

### Ignoring Prosody in Favor of Segments
The student may focus on getting individual sounds right while neglecting the overall rhythm and intonation of the language. In many cases, prosody matters more for intelligibility than individual segments. A sentence with all the right sounds but wrong intonation may sound foreign or even incomprehensible. Ensure that shadowing and reading-aloud practice attend to the music of the language, not just its individual notes.

### Treating Dialect as Distraction
When the student encounters speakers whose pronunciation differs from their learning materials, the temptation is to treat this as a problem. It is not. It is the reality of language. Every encounter with variation is a learning opportunity. The student who can understand multiple varieties has stronger comprehension than the student who has only trained on one.
